schools.summary: Cyril: knowing their malice he became their guest to benefit those present; Gregory: the dropsy in the one body images the disease in the Pharisees' minds.
They watched insidiously (periphrastic imperfect, middle voice); the host a 'ruler of the Pharisees,' likely of the Sanhedrin.
Calvin: the miracle to correct, not abolish, the Sabbath — God's works and charity do not violate its rest; Henry: he came eating and drinking, even with enemies, to do good.
'Dropsy' (hydropikos) a technical medical word, Luke's only record of this cure — the physician's precise diagnosis; debate whether the man was a planted trap or a sufferer come for help.
Jesus takes the initiative (unlike Luke 6/Matt 12), reading their thoughts and proposing the question first; the dilemma forestalls their charge; rabbinic rules allowed life-saving.
Calvin: the question — is healing a violation of the Sabbath? — asked to guard himself against their slander; Wesley: answering the thoughts rising in their hearts.
Their silence forfeits any later objection; 'took hold of him' (the only healing-use), cured by contact, then 'let him go' — dismissed him away from the critics.
From their own practice (cf Luke 13:15; Matt 12:11): if an ox may be saved on the Sabbath, much more a man; textual variant 'son or ox' / 'ass or ox' (look alike in uncials).
Calvin: he shows he did nothing against the Sabbath, chiefly to guard himself against their slanders, not to instruct the blinded.

themes: the ox fallen in the pit

schools.summary: They 'were powerless to answer'; helpless to take up the argument, hating to admit they cared more for an ox than for the dropsical man.
Gill: the justice and mercy of his reasoning, agreeing with their own practice, shut their mouths.
Ambrose: after the healing, humility taught; Theophylact: vainglory no slight disease; Bede reads the 'parable' of the Church, where none should boast his merits.
Cyril: to seek unfitting honours is rashness; the lowly are crowned; Basil on the order due at a feast.
'Parable' = precept/rule; they 'were picking out' the chief couches (imperfect); Calvin on the Pharisees' rife ambition and envy over seats.
The homely caution against claiming the chief couch lest displaced in shame; Christ honours ordinary courtesy yet aims chiefly to reprove pride; the Eastern reclining posture.

'Shalt begin with shame' — the public moment of embarrassment, forced to the lowest place; Ellicott meets the difficulty that the precept seems calculating, as social precepts rise from prudence to higher ground.
The lowest seat shows no greed for honour; the humble guest called up higher receives honour freely given; the host's 'Friend' a word of affection (John 15:14).
Cyril hears beneath it the table God spreads for his own (Ps 23:5).
A universal divine principle — God abases the proud and favours the lowly; a repeated saying (Luke 18:14; Matt 23:12), echoed in 1 Pet 5:5.
Calvin: not ordinary social fact but God as Judge, who humbles haughtiness (Jas 4:6) — God an enemy to the self-exalting.
Theophylact: guard the host against feasting to gain men's favour; Bede: kin and friends not forbidden, but such feasting fails to merit eternal life.
Calvin: not an absolute condemnation of entertaining friends — not to take away civility; Clarke: he condemns feasts given to the rich to flatter or to procure a return.
Robertson: present imperative prohibiting the habit of inviting only friends.
The four classes who cannot repay — poor, maimed, lame, blind (anarthrous, the whole helpless company); the form of obedience varies, best fulfilled by thoughtful, discriminate relief.
The first occurrence of 'resurrection' in Christ's teaching (Ellicott); the poor cannot repay, so God recompenses 'at the resurrection of the just' (cf Acts 23:6).
Calvin: blessed are those who give without expecting earthly reward, looking to God; the self-interested have no claim on God's reward.
Augustine: he sighs for a distant bliss while the Bread of the kingdom (John 6:51) lay before him — 'open not your mouth but your heart.'
Cyril: a carnal, careless hearer who thought the saints' reward bodily; Eusebius on supposing the resurrection one with the kingdom.
Robertson: a pious platitude (ignorance or hypocrisy), the rabbis' banquet-figure for heaven; the Jewish dream of a temporal Messianic feast.

The host = God, the supper = salvation, the invitation = the gospel offer; the first 'feast' imagery in Christ's teaching (later expanded in Matt 22); the conventional remark answered to show complacency is no guarantee of godliness.
Bede: the man represents God the Father.

The Eastern second summons when the feast is ready (to refuse it a deadly affront); the servant = the order of prophets and apostles; 'supper time' = the time of Christ's coming; 'all things now ready.'
Gill: the servant = John the Baptist, or Christ, or the gospel ministry proclaiming the kingdom at hand.
'With one consent' looked like a conspiracy, each begging off (paraiteisthai); the first plea absurd (inspecting land near nightfall); sinners plead a self-made necessity.
Wesley: the three pleas — will ('I go'), pretended necessity ('I must'), impossibility ('I cannot') — all perish by things in themselves lawful.
A trifling excuse — he could test the oxen any time; selfish, like all sinners; 'five yoke' within a moderate farmer's reach (cf 1 Kgs 19:19).

The curtest, rudest excuse ('I cannot come'); the law excused the newly married from war (Deut 24:5; 20:5-7) but not from courtesy — they treated the feast as a burdensome conscription; family love draws the heart from God.
The host's ingressive anger and haste ('quickly'); the righteous indignation stronger in Matt 22:6; the 'streets and lanes' gather the city's neglected — poor, maimed, halt, blind.
Gill: the first-bidden Jews having rejected the word, the call goes to the despised and outcast.
'Yet there is room' — Barnes: heaven not yet full, room for millions more; the Master wished the places filled; the Eastern larger hall borrowed for many guests.
The highways and hedges = the homeless, the Gentiles; 'compel' (anankason) means earnest persuasion, 'no thought of compulsion' (Clarke, Robertson).
Calvin: 'as it were, violence' = the urgency of love for the poor.
Wesley: compel 'with all the violence of love, and the force of God's word' — such compulsion only was used by Christ and his apostles.

debates.question: What does 'compel them to come in' mean?

sides.position: earnest persuasion / the constraint of love, not force

schools.summary: The Master's summing-up: not that no Jew is saved, but that those who decidedly rejected the offer shall not taste it; the anti-type in Israel's general rejection, yet not shutting the door on individuals who turn.
Gregory: great rewards reached only by great labours; Theophylact: many followed lukewarmly, so he shows what a disciple must be.
Robertson: 'he turned' a deliberate act to check the unthinking enthusiasm of crowds following just to follow; Henry: understand discipleship's terms before professing it.
'Hate' = love less (Matt 10:37; Rom 9:13 'Esau hated'); not literal, since parents are to be honoured (Matt 15:4); the hatred only where choice/competition comes in; here stronger ('not hating').
Wesley: love them comparatively to Christ, renouncing all when they compete with him.
Chrysostom: bearing the cross = ever having death before our eyes, dying daily; Basil joins it to baptism, where the old man is crucified.
The familiar figure (Luke 9:23; Matt 16:24); each bears his own cross as Jesus did his (the verb elsewhere only of Christ, John 19:17); a fresh weight as his own cross neared.

Gregory: every work preceded by consideration — brace against the world's ills; Theophylact: lay no foundation unfinished, lest like those who went back (John 6:66); Basil: the aim is to show the impossibility of pleasing God amid distractions.
Calvin: consider beforehand what the gospel demands — many yield to slight temptations because they pictured unmixed enjoyment.
Robertson: 'sit down' the attitude of deliberation, counting the cost first (cf Prov 24:27).
'To mock him' = to ridicule; the perfective 'finish out to the end' (ektelesai); the half-built tower exposed to public scorn.
Gill: the rash professor who takes up religion and a church-state, then fails.
The taunt 'this fellow began and was not able to finish'; the shame in the gap between promise and performance.
Gill: he set out well, promised great things, made a stir, then could not finish.
The king counts his troops as the builder his money; our enemies many and strong, our strength feeble; a possible side-glance at Herod's war with Aretas.
Wesley: the 'other king' perhaps the prince of this world, with greater numbers.

The general scope (not the details) is thoroughness — if unable to fight, make peace; 'conditions of peace' = 'the things that make for peace' (Luke 19:42); some see the sinner suing to God for terms.
'Renounceth not' (apotassetai, a military word) = say good-bye to all — possessions, ease, besetting sins, fancied righteousness (cf Phil 3:7-8).
Calvin: prefer Christ to life and all the flesh's wishes; Wesley: forsake all by withdrawing affection from the creatures, enjoying them only in and for God.
Bede: season the heart with the salt of spiritual wisdom, becoming the salt of the earth; Theophylact extends it to private individuals, useful to those around them.
Set beside Matt 5:13; Mark 9:50 — salt the purifying principle of unselfish devotion; Lightfoot reads it of the apostate who, counting the cost, goes back and becomes unsavoury salt.
A new use of the salt-figure (independent of Matt 5:13; Mark 9:50) — fit neither for soil nor dunghill, cast out; 'he that hath ears' another repeated saying (Luke 8:8; Matt 11:15).
Cyril hears in the 'ears' the new hearing given in baptism for the divine mysteries.

themes: fit for neither land nor dunghill
